coredata.dc:title: Rational design of novel carbon-oxygen quantum dots for ratiometrically mapping pH and reactive oxygen species scavenging
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Xin
Zhao, Lei
Hu, Jinshuang
Wei, Hua
Liu, Xiaoyang
Li, Enshou
Yang, Shenghong
coredata.dc:description: Heteroatom doping except oxygen is the main method to improve the performance of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), the application is also limited by unpredictable biological negative effects and environmental risks from heteroatoms (N/P/S, etc.). To maximize the role of oxygen while avoiding the negative effects of other heteroatoms, herein, we rationally designed and synthesized a new type of carbon-oxygen quantum dots (COQDs) with high fluorescent performance aim to realize pH sensing and reactive oxygen species (ROS) scavenging. The toxicity test results from four levels of bacterias, Hela cells, Daphnia Magna, and zebrafish indicate that the designed COQDs possess high biocompatibility and low environmental risk. Based on the opposing response of COQDs and Rhodamine 6G (Rh 6G) fluorescent signal to pH changes in the range of 6.0–9.0, a COQDs-Rh 6G complex was constructed by combining Rh 6G for ratiometric pH sensing, and a scanning colorimetric analysis model was further developed for the rapid pH judgment of commercially available waters. The proposed COQDs and pH sensing system were also successfully applied for bioimaging and intracellular pH analysis, respectively. More importantly, the designed COQDs also exhibit excellent ROS scavenging ability, which can be used as an effective antioxidant with good biocompatibility.
coredata.dcterms:subject: COQDs
High compatibility
pH sensing
Scanometric
ROS Scavenging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: pH sensing and reactive oxygen species scavenging
Biotoxicity test and bioimaging application of COQDs
Simple pH detection for real water samples and intracellular pH imaging

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We acknowledge the ﬁnancial support from the projects of National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21806189 ), the Youth Science and Technology Foundation of Gansu Province (No. 20JR10RA764 ), the Young Doctor Cooperative Foundation of Qilu University of Technology ( Shandong Academy of Sciences ) (No. 2019BSHZ0027 ) and the Program for Scientific Research Innovation Team in Colleges and Universities of Shandong Province.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Excitation (EX) and emission (EM) spectrum of COQDs (a), the quantum yield of designed COQDs (b), UV–Vis absorption spectrum of COQDs (c), high-resolution TEM image of COQDs (d), the XRD pattern (e) and FTIR spectrum (f) of COQDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The bacterial growth curve of COQDs (a), MTT experiment results of COQDs (b), survival rate for Daphnia Magna (c) and hatching rate for zebrafish embryos treated with different COQDs concentrations (d). Significant differences were obtained by Duncan comparison with p＜0.05. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Bright-field transmission images and fluorescent microscopy images (under UV light excitation) of Hela cells (a, e), Daphnia Magna (b, f), Zebrafish embryos (c, g) and Zebrafish larvae (d, h) treated with COQDs (10 μg mL−1). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
The spectral response of COQDs-Rh 6G (a), COQDs (b), and Rh 6G to pH changes (c). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

PL spectra of COQDs-Rh 6G aqueous solutions with different pH values (a), The linear relationship between the PL intensity ratio values of the system and the changes in pH value (b). Reversibility determination of COQDs-Rh 6G spectrum changes (c). Plots of the ratio value of B/(G + R) vs the pH. Inset: Photographs of COQDs-Rh 6G with various pH from 5.94 to 9.7 (d). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Hela cell fluorescent images of COQDs-Rh 6G at pH 6.0 (a), 7.5 (b) and 9.0 (c) excited at UV light excitation. Images of blue channel (420–480 nm) (a-1) (b-1) (c-1); images of green channel (500–560 nm) (a-2) (b-2) (c-2). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The scavenging activity of COQDs to ·OH (a), O2·− (b), DPPH (c). Cytoprotective effect of COQDs against H2O2 induced toxicity of Hela cells (d). Cells were incubated with COQDs (0, 50, 100 μg mL−1) for 24 h and then treated with 0.3 and 0.6 mM H2O2 for 90 min. Cells without treatment were used as control. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Intracellular ROS generation evaluation by DCFH-DA. DCFH fluorescence images are excited by blue excitation. Hela cells treated with DCFH-DA only were used as a negative control (a). Hela cells treated with H2O2 were used as positive control groups (b). Green fluorescence indicates the presence of ROS, intracellular ROS generation was illustrated for pre-treated Hela cells with 50 (c), 100 (d) and 200 μg mL−1 (e) COQDs. Normalized intensities of Hela cells (f). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Illustration of the biocompatibility and low environmental risk of prepared COQDs (a), free radical scavenging activity of COQDs and the designed COQDs-Rh 6G complex for ratiometric pH sensing (b).
Determination of pH in actual tap water and drink samples through scan-colorimetric method.

head.ce:title: Rational design of novel carbon-oxygen quantum dots for ratiometrically mapping pH and reactive oxygen species scavenging

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Molecular Engineering
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Heteroatom doping except oxygen is the main method to improve the performance of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), the application is also limited by unpredictable biological negative effects and environmental risks from heteroatoms (N/P/S, etc.). To maximize the role of oxygen while avoiding the negative effects of other heteroatoms, herein, we rationally designed and synthesized a new type of carbon-oxygen quantum dots (COQDs) with high fluorescent performance aim to realize pH sensing and reactive oxygen species (ROS) scavenging. The toxicity test results from four levels of bacterias, Hela cells, Daphnia Magna, and zebrafish indicate that the designed COQDs possess high biocompatibility and low environmental risk. Based on the opposing response of COQDs and Rhodamine 6G (Rh 6G) fluorescent signal to pH changes in the range of 6.0–9.0, a COQDs-Rh 6G complex was constructed by combining Rh 6G for ratiometric pH sensing, and a scanning colorimetric analysis model was further developed for the rapid pH judgment of commercially available waters. The proposed COQDs and pH sensing system were also successfully applied for bioimaging and intracellular pH analysis, respectively. More importantly, the designed COQDs also exhibit excellent ROS scavenging ability, which can be used as an effective antioxidant with good biocompatibility.

ce:section.ce:para: With the development of biomedical technology and the demand for precision medicine, cell morphology has become an important indicator of medical testing [1]. The adjustment and homeostasis of pH are essential for the growth of cells, and small fluctuations of pH may have great negative effects on the growth of plants and animals [2,3]. Abnormal pH can cause abnormal cell apoptosis and necrosis and even lead to cardiopulmonary and neurological diseases such as cancer and Alzheimer's [4,5]. Hence, accurate monitoring of intracellular pH can provide key information for various cell functions and pathological processes and is of great significance for the early diagnosis and diseases prevention. The fluorescent method combined microscopy imaging technology provides an excellent platform for the real-time monitoring of intracellular pH fluctuations with the advantages of fast response, high spatial and temporal resolution, non-invasiveness, high sensitivity, and so on [6,7]. As the core of the fluorescent method, conventional pH-sensitive fluorescent materials such as organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots have greatly limited their practicality due to tedious synthetic steps, time-consuming, or high biological toxicity and environmental risk [8,9]. Therefore, the development of new fluorescent materials with pH-sensitive and high biological and environmental compatibility is of great significance for biomedical applications.

As another important aspect of biomedical, reactive oxygen species (ROS) are widely involved in tissue injury and lead to chronic inflammation [10–12]. So efficient ROS scavenging is considered important means to treat acute kidney and liver injury, or other wounds caused by inflammation [13,14]. Liu et al. have synthesized a kind of ultra-small Cu5.4O nanoparticles with high efficient ROS scavenging capacity, which can overcome the low stability and efficacy, and poor bioavailability of small molecular ROS scavenging agents [15]. Unfortunately, these metal-based nano-antioxidants usually suffer from safety concerns because of uncontrollable leakage and residues of metal ions [16]. Hence, it is significant to develop metal-free nano-antioxidants with great ROS scavenging activity, high biocompatibility, and low environmental risk.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a new type of luminescent nanomaterials, provides new opportunities for biomedical applications based on variety of synthesis methods and the fascinating characteristics of excellent biocompatibility, small dimension, multiphoton excitation, outstanding light stability, and easy operation [17–20]. The abundant surface-active groups of CQDs make it extremely suitable as a new type of fluorescent probe for sensitive pH analysis in cells [21,22], and it also provides the possibility for the antioxidant application due to the adjustment and modifiable properties of CQDs [23,24]. However, it is still a great challenge to synthesize the CQDs with both pH-sensitive and antioxidant performance so far.

The synthesis of CQDs has been intensively researched for many years, and the doping of heteroatoms other than oxygen has become the main means to improve its performance [25]. From the initial nitrogen, sulfur, and phosphorus, to the use of metal ions doping (copper, zinc, germanium, terbium, etc.) [26,27]. Although the doping of nitrogen and other heteroatoms can effectively improve the photoluminescence (PL) performance of CQDs, the uncontrollable local biological damage and environmental risk should be attracted more concern. For instance, a large amount of nitrogen/phosphorus-doped CQDs discharged into the water may cause eutrophication and damage the water ecosystem [28]. Nitrogen and nitrogen-containing substances can be transformed into carcinogenic nitrites in the soil, which can be absorbed by the body through food and further harming human health. The toxicity of heavy metals makes heavy metal-doped CQDs completely abandon their original advantages of good biocompatibility and limit their applications in biological analysis [29,30]. Encouragingly, even at a high dose, Chong et al. have proven that the enriched oxygenated groups functionalized CQDs possess high compatibility both in vitro and in vivo due to its high oxygen content and ultra-small size [31,32]. Unfortunately, the problem of low PL efficiency and availability of pure oxygen-doped CQDs is still not well solved [33].

Considering the demands of biomedical and bottlenecks in the synthesis and application of CQDs, in this work, we rationally designed a new type of carbon-oxygen quantum dots (COQDs) with both pH-sensitive and antioxidant performance, which was synthesized through hydrothermal treatment of resorcinol and ascorbic acid. The proposed COQDs not only can maximize the role of oxygen but also can avoid unpredictable biological negative effects and environmental risks effectively caused by other heteroatoms such as N/P/S, etc. We systematically studied the toxicity of COQDs from four levels of bacteria, Hela cells, Daphnia Magna, and zebrafish, which indicate the high compatibility suitable for bioimaging application. Importantly, the PL intensity of prepared COQDs shows a highly sensitive response to pH in the range of 6.06–9.18. For the simple and accurate application, a COQDs-Rh 6G complex was constructed by combining Rhodamine 6G (Rh 6G) for ratiometric pH sensing, and a scanning colorimetric analysis model was developed for rapid judgment (Scheme 1 ). The designed system was successfully applied for the pH detection of water samples and monitoring pH fluctuations of living cells. Furthermore, the antioxidative activity of prepared COQDs was confirmed through free radical scavenging experiment including O2·−, ·OH and DPPH. We expect that this study could facilitate the development of high-efficiency pure oxygen-doped CQDs to realize reasonable and low-risk biomedical applications.

Benzenediol, ascorbic acid, rhodamine 6G, salicylic acid, pyrogallol, Tris-HCl, and DPPH (2,2-di(4-tert-octylphenyl)-1-picrylhydrazyl) were purchased from Aladdin Biological Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4) and citric acid were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). All other reagents were of analytical reagent grade and used without any further purification. Ultrapure water was used throughout the whole experiment with a resistivity of 18.25 MΩ cm (UPR-II-40L, Sichuan, China).

The PL spectra of COQDs were obtained by an F97Pro fluorescent spectrophotometer (Shanghai Lengguang Technology Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China). The ultraviolet–visible absorption spectra of COQDs were analyzed by UV2800S UV–visible spectrophotometer (Shanghai Hengping Scientific Instrument Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China). The morphology of the prepared COQDs was characterized by the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F30, USA) with a 200 kV accelerating voltage. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) was measured by BI-200SM (Brookhaven, USA) to characterize the particle size distribution of COQDs. The element composition and molecular structure of COQDs was measured by a NEXUS 670 Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet, USA) and an EscaLab Xi + X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). X-ray diﬀraction (XRD) spectrum was recorded using an D8-ADVANCE diﬀractometer (Bruker, Germany). The MTT test was performed on an RT-6100 enzyme-mark analyzer (Shenzhen, China). The fluorescent imaging photographs of cell, Daphnia Magna, and zebrafish were performed using BX53 fluorescence microscope (Olympus, Japan). Organic element analysis were analyzed by Elemantar (Vario EL cube, Germany).

The synthesis of COQDs was optimized systemically and carried out using the hydrothermal method. In a typical process, 0.37 g resorcinol and 0.05g ascorbic acid were dissolved in 20 mL water. Then, the resulted mixture was heated hydrothermally in a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave at 200 °C for 6 h. After cooled down to room temperature, the products were collected and dialyzed (molecular weight cut-off 1000) to remove unreacted small molecules and oligomers. Then the products were dried in a vacuum drying chamber with temperature of 40 °C, dark brown solid can be obtained, which is verified to be COQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH sensing and reactive oxygen species scavenging
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical assay, the COQDs powder was dispersed in dimethyl sulfoxide as a stock solution (40 mg L−1), and then 300 μL stock solution of COQDs and 4 μL Rh 6G (1.0 mM) was mixed with 2.7 mL NaH2PO4-Citric acid buffer solution with different pH. After 5 min of incubation, the PL emission spectra of the complex probe were recorded with an excitation wavelength of 350 nm at room temperature. The single COQDs pH sensing was performed through similar procedures. The reactive oxygen species scavenging experiments are measured according to standard methods, and the detailed content can be found in Supporting Information.

For pH sensing and antioxidant applications while avoiding the negative effects of heteroatoms other than oxygen, we designed a new type of carbon-oxygen quantum dots (COQDs) through hydrothermal treatment of resorcinol and ascorbic acid, which can maximize the availability and effectiveness of oxygen-containing functional groups. Phenolic compounds possess both weak acid and antioxidant effects, which can be as efficient precursor candidate for synthesizing functional COQDs. Herein, benzenediol, as representative phenolic compounds, was used to react with ascorbic acid for synthesizing COQDs. The prepared COQDs from resorcinol exhibit higher PL performance than that of catechol and hydroquinone, as shown in Fig. S1. The participation of ascorbic acid can prevent oxidation of phenols during the reaction process and improve the PL efficiency of designed COQDs to realize more suitable for biosensing meanwhile. As shown in Fig. S2a, after ascorbic acid is involved, the quantum yield (QY) of COQDs can be improved up to 6.6 times. The influence of reaction conditions including temperature and time on the PL performance of products was also studied systemically, and the optimal reaction temperature and time are 200 °C and 6 h, respectively (Figs. S2b and c).

Although without the surface passivation or doping of heteroatoms other than oxygen, the QY of prepared COQDs under optimal synthesis conditions (using Quinine sulfate as a standard) still can reach 42.9% (Fig. 1 b), which is comparable or even higher than that of the previous reported N or other heteroatoms-doped CQDs (Table S1). Organic element analysis results shown that the content of C, O and H is 64.72%, 29.22% and 4.90%, respectively, and other elements content is lower than 1.2%, which suggested that the synthesized COQDs with highly oxygen-contained. Furthermore, TEM image indicated that the formation of large amounts of ultra-small COQDs with a size of 1.7–3.1 nm (Fig. S3). The above results showed that COQDs with high PL performance and good morphology has been successfully prepared without heteroatoms doping other than oxygen.

The emission peak of COQDs is centered at 440 nm with brightly blue fluorescence under optimal excitation wavelength of 360 nm (Fig. 1a). Notably, the self-absorption of COQDs is quite low because of small overlap between excitonic absorption and emission spectrum, which is beneficial for efficient fluorescence emission. As shown in Fig. S4, when the excitation wavelengths shift from 280 nm to 400 nm, the emission of COQDs is almost independent of excitation, which is attributed to the relatively uniform size distribution and surface state of COQDs [34]. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of COQDs was shown in Fig. 1c, peaks at 275 nm and 480 nm can be assigned to the π-π∗ and n-π∗ transitions, respectively [35,36].

The microstructure of prepared COQDs was further characterized by high-resolution TEM. The results in Fig. 1d revealed the high crystallinity of the COQDs with lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which matches with the (100) diffraction plane of graphitic carbon [37,38]. The XRD pattern of the COQDs (Fig. 1e) displays an obvious peak centered at 24° similar to the reported CQDs, which confirms their high crystalline graphene structure [39]. The characteristic absorption of functional groups including 3446.2 cm−1, 1621.8 cm−1, was shown in FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1f), which corresponded to the presence of –OH and CC groups on the surface of COQDs [40,41]. The XPS spectra of the COQDs (Fig. S5) further confirmed the existence of C–C/CC and C–OH [42]. These results suggest that the designed COQDs only contain C, O, and H three elements, and the surface has abundant oxygen-contained functional groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biotoxicity test and bioimaging application of COQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The level of biotoxicity is an important indicator to evaluate whether a material can be applied in bio-related conditions. However, most of biotoxicity evaluation of CQDs still stays on the cellular level [43–45], which is difficult to reflect its impact on the actual environmental system. In order to promote the suitable application of prepared COQDs, we studied the biotoxicity of COQDs systemically from four levels. Firstly, we performed bacterial growth experiments and MTT experiments to evaluate the toxicity of COQDs using E. coli and Hela cells as model. Fig. 2 a shows that even if the concentration of COQDs reaches 50 μg mL−1, it still cannot cause any negative effects on bacterial growth. The MTT experiment results shown (Fig. 2b) that when the COQDs concentration is up to 500 μg mL−1, the cell survival rate is still more than 80%, which indicated that the prepared COQDs have excellent biocompatibility and are suitable for biological systems.

Furthermore, the survival rate of Daphnia Magna and hatching rate of zebrafish embryos was used to evaluate the toxicity of COQDs in the practical environmental ecosystem. As shown in Fig. 2c, the survival rate of Daphnia Magna is still as high as 60% even the concentration reaches 100 μg mL−1, and the LC50 of COQDs is about 134.9 μg mL−1 (95% Cl = 114.4–158.1 μg mL−1), which is much lower than the acute toxicity of other carbon nanomaterials, such as fullerenes and carbon nanotubes (0.2–50 μg mL−1) [46,47]. When the concentration of COQDs is less than 100 μg mL−1, the zebrafish embryos can hatch normally (Fig. 2d). According to the Acute Toxicity Rating Scale of the Fish and Wildlife Service, when the LC50 value is > 100 μg mL−1, they are classified as “virtually non-toxic” [48]. All above results indicated that the proposed COQDs possesses lower toxicity and higher environmental compatibility. As a negative control, the CNQDs were synthesized using m-phenylenediamine replace resorcinol. As shown in Fig. S6, the toxicity of CNQDs to Daphnia Magna and zebrafish embryos is apparently higher than that of COQDs.

The proposed COQDs was further applied for bioimaging because of its excellent biocompatibility and low environmental risk. From Fig. 3 we can see that the prepared COQDs can easily get into Hela cells, Daphnia Magna, zebrafish embryo and larvae, and emit bright blue fluorescence. It is worth mentioning that the concentration for imaging is much lower than toxic effect concentration, hence the proposed COQDs can be classified as a high-safety nanomaterial due its high-efficiency PL performance and low toxicity.

Combining the low-risk and abundant oxygen-contained functional groups, the prepared COQDs is ideal for pH sensing. Firstly, we studied the stability of COQDs systemically, as shown in Fig. S7, the prepared COQDs exhibits good tolerance against salt and photobleaching. Satisfactorily, the COQDs are extremely sensitive to pH, and can be as an effective sensor for the pH monitoring. From Figs. S8a and b we can see that the COQDs solution can only emit weak fluorescence with pH of 3.5–6.0, while exhibiting bright fluorescence with pH above 9.0. Within the pH range of 6.06–9.18, the PL intensity of COQDs increases linearly by 7 times, and the correlation coefficient is 0.9963 (Fig. S8c). Besides, the PL switching response of COQDs can cycle 9 times at least (Fig. S8d). The high sensitivity of COQDs fluorescence signal to pH should be attributed to the protonation/deprotonation of hydroxyl on the surface of COQDs. The zeta potentials of COQDs at pH 4, 7.5 and 10 are −3.56 mV, −6.18 mV, and −11.1 mV, respectively; which is a proof of above sensing mechanism. Compared with other CQDs-based pH sensor, the proposed system mainly possesses double advantages: firstly, the prepared COQDs shown low biological toxicity and environmental risk, and is more suitable for reasonable and extensive applications; secondly, with an appropriate response range and high sensitivity, the pH sensing system can be used to efficiently monitor intracellular pH fluctuations.

Although the COQDs can be used to pH sensing directly with high sensitivity, however, the single-color intensity signal change is difficult for visual detection because the human eye is low sensitivity to changes in brightness. Therefore, we further construct a hybrid ratiometric fluorescent probe based on COQDs and Rh 6G (COQDs-Rh 6G) through electrostatic interaction. The complex probe not only can exhibit superior sensing performance than COQDs, but also can realize color change that can be distinguished by the naked eye. As shown in Fig. 4 b and c, the fluorescence properties of Rh 6G under different pH are opposite to the COQD prepared by us. From Fig. 4a we can see that the COQDs-Rh 6G complex show obvious dual-emission characteristics with wavelengths of 440 nm and 560 nm under the excitation of 350 nm. With pH changes from 5.5 to 10.0, the PL intensity of 560 nm decreased and that of 440 nm increased sharply, and the fluorescence color changes from yellow to bright blue, which can be recognized easily by the naked eye.

As shown in Fig. 4b, when the pH is 5.5, COQDs can only emit weak fluorescence; and COQDs can emit bright blue fluorescence with the pH increases to 10.0. Significantly, after COQDs and Rh 6G are compounded, the PL intensity of Rh 6G changes more obviously than single Rh 6G with the change of pH, and the change rates before and after compounding are 10% and 57%, respectively, which further suggest the superiority of the composite sensing system. The increase of change extent of Rh 6G PL intensity is mainly due to the inner filtering effect caused by the increment in the visible light absorption signal of COQDs after the pH rises. As shown in Fig. S9, the UV–Vis absorption intensity at 480 nm of COQDs increased with pH changes from 5.5 to 10 due the deprotonation [49]. This phenomenon not only can improve the fluorescent of COQDs, but also can lead to further decrease in Rh 6G PL intensity based on the effective overlap between the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of COQDs and emission spectrum of Rh 6G. Besides, we also measured the acid dissociation constants (pKa) of COQDs and COQD-Rh 6G, which is 7.45 and 7.40, respectively. The pKa values of them are very close, which explains why the pH response interval of COQDs and COQDs-Rh 6G is basically the same.

We also systematically studied the stability of the complex pH sensor. As shown in Fig. S10a, even in a high concentration (0.5 M) NaCl solution, the fluorescence ratio can also be well maintained. It can be seen from Fig. S10b that even if the temperature reaches 45 °C, it can still maintain a stable ratio signal. As shown in Fig. S10c, with the continuous excitation light for 4 h, the PL intensity ratio of I440nm and I560nm cannot change significantly. Moreover, taking into account the complexity of the intracellular environment, we also determined the interference of potential metal cations. Fig. S10d show that common cations have no obvious interference to the signal. The results show that COQDs-Rh 6G complex possesses good ratio fluorescent stability, which is essential for practical sensing applications [50,51]. Therefore, the constructed COQDs-Rh 6G complex can be as an effective pH sensor for realizing the visual monitoring of pH changes.

We further explored the linear relationship between pH change and fluorescent intensity ratio, which was described as I440nm/I560nm. In Fig. 5 a, with the increase of pH, the PL intensity of the system at 440 nm increases, while the PL intensity at 560 nm decreases. It can be seen that the ratio value of I440nm/I560nm gradually increases with the improvement of alkalinity, and show an excellent linear relationship in the range of pH 6.0–9.3 (Fig. 5b). The linear correlation coefficient can reach 0.9936, and the fluorescence color of the solution changes from yellow to yellow-white last turns to bright blue. The ratio value changes can reach up to 14 times, which is more sensitive than previous reports, as shown in Table S2. In addition, the COQDs-Rh 6G pH sensing system also shows excellent reversibility (Fig. 5c). Even if concentrated sodium hydroxide solution and hydrochloric acid are used in the same solution, and the pH response of the solution can still repeat for eight cycles between 5.5 and 10, which indicated the robustness of proposed system.

The scanometric analysis method has been considered the easiest way to achieve quick quantitative analysis in recent years, which has significant value for on-site and rapid detection because it gets rid of the dependence on complicated equipments [52–54]. In our work, we also fabricate an effective scanometric quantitative model based on the color change of COQDs-Rh 6G complex. The fluorescence images were taken by using a common smartphone under UV light and the RGB values was read through an APP (Color Measure developed by PotatotreeSoft). In Fig. 5d, the ratio value of B to the sum of G and R was calculated to digitize the differences of fluorescence change under various pH conditions. The ratio values have a good linear relationship with pH within the range of 5.94–9.7 (R2 = 0.9942), and the color change can be recognized intuitively (Fig. 5d inset). Based on the above results, we can infer that the developed the ratiometric sensing system not can realize rapid quantitative analysis of pH, but also can applied for monitoring the intracellular pH fluctuations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simple pH detection for real water samples and intracellular pH imaging
ce:section.ce:para: Consider the simplicity of proposed scan-colorimetric quantitative method, which was used to analyze the pH of practical water samples conveniently. Several commercially available water samples were selected for verifying the feasibility of proposed method. As shown in Table 1 , the determination results of scan-colorimetric quantitative method are consistent with that of pH-meter and the relative standard deviation (RSD) values based on three parallel determination results are lower than 2.7%, which shows that the method has good effectiveness and practicability.

Two representatives ROS, ·OH and O2·−, and DPPH were selected to study the free radical scavenging activities of COQDs. As shown in Fig. 7a and b, COQDs show high ROS scavenging activity, when the concentration of COQDs reaches 60 μg mL−1, more than 90% of ·OH is removed, and COQDs of 130 μg mL−1 can decompose about 90% of O2·−. In order to further confirm the antioxidative activity of COQDs, we performed H-atom donating ability experiment by used the classic DPPH assay. As shown in Fig.7c and 150 μg mL−1 COQDs can eliminate more than 80% of free radicals. Moreover, the scavenging ability of designed COQDs is similar or even stronger to which have been reported heteroatom-doped CQDs, such as N,SCDs [55], SeCQDs [56], FCQDs [57] and Ce-CNDs [58]. We also evaluated the antioxidant activity of CNQDs prepared by m-phenylenediamine and ascorbic acid, as shown in Fig. S12, the ROS free radical scavenging activity of CNQDs is much lower than COQDs.

The ROS scavenging activity was further examined by studying the cytoprotective effect of the as-prepared COQDs against H2O2 induced damage on Hela cells. As shown in Fig. 7d, the cell survival rate can be improved significantly after involving COQDs when Hela cells were exposed to H2O2 with different concentrations. The result indicates that COQDs can effectively protect cells from hydrogen peroxide-induced oxidative stress. Furthermore, DCFH-DA assay also was performed to monitor the intercellular ROS inhibition [59]. When ROS is present, DCFH-DA can be oxidized to form DCFH oxidation product with green fluorescence. As shown in Fig. 8 a, there is almost no fluorescence in the control group. After the addition of H2O2 in the cell medium, the generated ROS induced a significant increase in the green fluorescence (Fig. 8b). Conversely, the fluorescence intensity of pretreatment cells with COQDs reduced gradually with increasing the concentrations of COQDs, suggesting a lower level of intercellular ROS (Fig. 8c–f). All above results illustrated designed COQDs has excellent intercellular ROS scavenging ability.

The prefect antioxidant ability of COQDs may be derived from polyphenol-like structures with natural antioxidant property. The participation of ascorbic acid can not only improve the fluorescent efficiency for realizing reasonable sensing application, but also can prevent resorcinol during the process of polymerization and carbonization to form COQDs from being oxidized. As previous reported, the phenolic H-atom can be effectively captured by free radical and form intermediate phenoxyl radical, which is further coupled with other free radicals to achieve resonance stabilization through p-π orbital overlap [60,61]. Furthermore, the C–OH species on the graphene structure have been proven to possess phenol-like property [62]. Therefore, the COQDs antioxidant with phenol-like groups can act as a potentially efficient H atom donor to capture ROS to cope with chronic inflammation.

In summary, a new type of functional COQDs was designed for sensitive pH analysis and high-efficiency ROS scavenging, which can maximize the role of oxygen while avoiding the negative effects of other heteroatoms. The QY of prepared COQDs can still reach more than 40% without the aid of heteroatoms other than oxygen, breaking the dependence of high PL CQDs on nitrogen and other heteroatoms. The proposed COQDs possess high compatibility and low environmental risk, which was successfully applied for the bioimaging of Hela cell, Daphnia Magna, zebrafish embryos and larvae. Based on the opposing response of COQDs and Rh 6G fluorescent signal to pH changes, we constructed a high-sensitive COQDs-Rh 6G complex ratiometric pH sensing system for simpler and intuitive determination of pH. Furthermore, relying on the phenol-like groups, the COQDs also exhibit well free radical scavenging activity and have the potential to be a new highly effective antioxidant. Due to the high compatibility, low environmental risk, and availability of proposed COQDs, we envision that this study can provide valuable reference for the application of CQDs-based fluorescent nanomaterials in biomedical.

Xin Wang: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Lei Zhao: Validation. Jinshuang Hu: Investigation. Hua Wei: Methodology. Xiaoyang Liu: Data curation. Enshou Li: Writing – review & editing. Shenghong Yang: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the ﬁnancial support from the projects of National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21806189), the Youth Science and Technology Foundation of Gansu Province (No. 20JR10RA764), the Young Doctor Cooperative Foundation of Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences) (No. 2019BSHZ0027) and the Program for Scientific Research Innovation Team in Colleges and Universities of Shandong Province.
